findings.message: The orphan-detection loop silently drops any signal entry whose `type_name` cannot be converted to `CatalogueEntryKey` (`Err(_) => { ... }`). That branch is reachable because `type_signals_codec::decode` accepts arbitrary `type_name` strings, so a malformed `<layer>-type-signals.json` can contain an uncovered entry that the gate never reports. This makes the gate fail open instead of blocking or surfacing an error.

The `None` branch swallows every `SignalReadFailed`, but `check_layer()` also raises that error for malformed signal documents (for example, invalid `type_name` values are converted to `SignalReadFailed` here). As a result, a corrupt or unreadable layer can be treated as if it simply had no document, and all-layers mode can return `Passed` instead of surfacing the failure.

All-layers skip logic keys off the human-readable `message` inside `PreReviewGateError::SignalReadFailed` instead of a typed not-found variant. Because that diagnostic is documented as opaque, a real read/decode failure can be misclassified as a missing document and silently skipped, which breaks the fail-closed behavior in the `None` branch.

`None` mode decides whether to skip a layer by parsing the opaque `PreReviewGateError::SignalReadFailed.message` string. That makes control flow depend on diagnostic text instead of a typed not-found signal, so a genuine read/decode failure can be misclassified as "missing" and silently skipped, letting all-layers mode pass when it should fail closed.

P1: `None`-layer mode now depends on `read_optional_signals()`, but this file only adds a default shim that calls `read_signals()`. The production filesystem adapter still reports a missing `*-type-signals.json` as `SignalReadFailed`, so the new all-layers path will still abort on absent layers instead of skipping them silently. The added passing test only uses a mock that overrides the new hook, so it does not cover the real adapter path.

This adapter reads `<items_dir>/<track_id>/task-contract.json` directly with `std::fs::read` and never rejects symlinks on the leaf or ancestor chain. A symlinked track directory or file can redirect the pre-review gate to attacker-controlled content; the existing filesystem loaders in this repo use `symlink_metadata`/`reject_symlinks_below` and fail closed here.

This adapter follows the same unchecked read path for `<items_dir>/<track_id>/<layer>-type-signals.json`. Because there is no boundary symlink rejection before `std::fs::read`, a symlinked `track_id` directory or signal file can bypass the intended real-file semantics used by the other infrastructure readers.

Reject `self.items_dir` itself before the below-root walk. `reject_symlinks_below(&path, &self.items_dir)` only checks descendants; if `--items-dir` is a symlink, `task-contract.json` can be read through the redirected root. `FsSpecFileLoader` shows the required fail-closed trusted_root check.

Reject `self.items_dir` itself before the below-root walk. `reject_symlinks_below(&path, &self.items_dir)` only checks descendants; if `--items-dir` is a symlink, `*-type-signals.json` can be read through the redirected root. `FsSpecFileLoader` shows the required fail-closed trusted_root check.

`entries` is deserialized as a plain `BTreeMap`, so duplicate task IDs in `task-contract.json` will be accepted with last-write-wins semantics during `serde_json::from_slice`. That silently drops contracted entries and can make the pre-review gate evaluate a different contract than the file actually encodes. Other strict infrastructure codecs in this repo reject duplicate map keys explicitly; this codec should fail closed too.

`FsTaskContractReader` reads `task-contract.json` with `std::fs::read` after the symlink check, but there is no file-size bound before loading the whole artifact into memory. The infrastructure review policy treats unbounded adapter reads as correctness issues: a malformed or unexpectedly large track artifact can force this gate path to allocate arbitrarily. Add a metadata length check or a bounded reader and map oversize files to `TaskContractReadFailed` before decoding.

`FsImplCatalogSignalReader` likewise loads `<layer>-type-signals.json` with unbounded `std::fs::read`. These signal files are adapter-boundary inputs, so the reader should enforce a maximum byte size before allocation/read and return `SignalReadFailed` on oversize input; otherwise a large artifact can exhaust memory during the pre-review gate.

`items_dir` is caller/config supplied, but this adapter treats it as the trusted root and joins `track_id` under it without canonicalizing the resolved path against an independent workspace trusted_root. The symlink guard only verifies descendants below `items_dir`; it does not prove the requested `task-contract.json` remains inside the repository trust boundary. This violates the trusted-root/CN-04 fail-closed pattern used for path inputs such as safe briefing paths.

`items_dir` is caller/config supplied, but the signal reader builds `<items_dir>/<track_id>/<layer>-type-signals.json` and then uses `items_dir` itself as the trust anchor. Without canonicalizing against an independent trusted workspace root, a configured items directory outside the repo can make the adapter read signal files outside the intended trust boundary; the descendant symlink checks do not close that gap.

`symlink_metadata(&path)` is used as the only presence probe before returning `Ok(None)`. That probe can traverse symlinked parent components, so a symlinked `track_id` directory that points outside the trusted root but has no leaf file is classified as â€śmissingâ€ť instead of being rejected. This weakens the boundary check; use `reject_symlinks_below` (or an equivalent fail-closed check) before treating `NotFound` as absence.

`TaskContractDocument::new` documents that a contract with no attributed entries is invalid, but it only rejects an empty map. A value like `{ T001: vec![] }` is accepted even though it contains zero `ContractedEntryRef`s, so the domain type can represent the fail-closed-empty contract the constructor claims to reject. Tighten the constructor to reject no actual attributions, and likely empty per-task lists as well.

`Blocked` is documented as carrying one or more gate violations, but the public variant stores a raw `Vec<PreReviewGateViolation>`, so callers can construct `PreReviewGateOutcome::Blocked { violations: vec![] }`. That makes an illegal gate state representable; follow the projectâ€™s non-empty findings pattern with a validated non-empty wrapper or constructor.

P1: `PreReviewGateOutcome::blocked()` validates that `violations` is non-empty, but the `Blocked { violations: Vec<_> }` variant is public and can still be constructed directly with `vec![]`. That means the returned type does not encode the invariant the constructor just checked, so the non-empty gate result can be bypassed at the API boundary.

`PreReviewGateOutcome::Blocked` no longer matches the tracked domain contract: the variant lost `#[non_exhaustive]` and its payload type changed from `Vec<PreReviewGateViolation>` to the new `PreReviewGateViolations` wrapper. That diverges from `track/items/pre-review-task-contract-conformance-gate-2026-06-27/domain-types.json` and breaks consumers generated against the declared shape.

`PreReviewGateOutcome::Blocked` no longer matches the declared domain contract: `domain-types.json` says the payload is `Vec<PreReviewGateViolation>` (and marks the variant non-exhaustive), but this implementation exposes the new public `PreReviewGateViolations` wrapper instead. That breaks shape parity for generated consumers and makes the domain API diverge from the catalog the rest of the track is built against.

The ADR limits the new `task-contract.json` validation to referential integrity, but the pre-review gate only checks entries that a task declares. That leaves an unattributed catalogue entry invisible to the new gate, so a Red/Yellow `impl_catalog` signal can still be deferred to merge-gate, contradicting the stated shift-left guarantee for contract violations. The plan needs a completeness invariant for relevant catalogue entries, not just RI of declared entries.

The ADR includes shape-only stubs in the stated shift-left problem, saying unimplemented code with only the shape in place should be caught earlier, but D3/D5 later define the new gate as impl_catalog blue only and explicitly state correct-signature stubs pass because body/liveness is reviewer-owned. That makes the plan internally inconsistent about the gate's guarantee and can lead implementation to expect body-aware blocking that the decision forbids. Remove stubs from the shift-left target/benefit, or change the decision to include a body-aware check.

The ADR frontmatter records every decision D1-D5 as `status: accepted`, but the README index adds this ADR as `Proposed`. That makes the plan artifacts contradict each other about whether this baseline ADR is accepted; update the index status to match the ADR's accepted decisions.

T002's orphan-detection step is backwards: it only walks `ContractedEntryRef`s already present in `task-contract.json`, so it cannot detect scope entries that were never attributed. `IN-03`/`AC-02` require comparing the contract against the full in-scope catalogue entry set, so as written this step cannot satisfy the orphan-entry case (and T007(b) is impossible to implement literally).

AC-05 is marked covered by T002/T007, but those tasks only create and test the conformance summary text; the implementation plan routes successful checks to stdout and never adds a task that appends the verified-conformance line to the reviewer briefing required by IN-06/AC-05. As written, the plan can complete with no briefing update, so reviewers would not receive the promised scope-of-guarantee notice.

This catalogue says the gate verifies all catalogue entries contracted to the track, while the spec and OQ-1 resolution define the gate as review-scope-specific via `cmd.group` and `<group>-type-signals.json`. If implemented from this contract, one scope review could be blocked by unrelated layers, defeating the per-scope pre-review gate design.

The plan makes `--group` do double duty as both the review scope and a TDDD layer: OQ-1 says the gate reads the TypeSignalsDocument for `cmd.group`, filters `ContractedEntryRef.layer == cmd.group`, and T008 then prepends that same gate to every `track-local-review-fix-codex` invocation. But the type catalogue models `group` as `ReviewGroupName` while the signal reader needs a `LayerId`, and review groups include non-layer scopes such as `plan-artifacts`. With the current universal wiring, those review runs will fail closed before the reviewer can start. The plan needs an explicit review-scope-to-layer mapping or a defined bypass for non-layer scopes before T008 is executable.

`task_contract_check` is specified to build the filesystem adapters from the CLI `items_dir` and call the use case directly, while the same plan/catalogue makes `TaskContractDriver` the primary adapter responsible for parsing `TaskContractInput` and rendering `PreReviewGateOutcome` to `CommandOutcome`. That leaves two conflicting execution paths: using `task_contract_driver()` ignores the `--items-dir` value because it wires a default path, while using `task_contract_check()` bypasses the primary adapter and duplicates driver responsibilities in the composition root. The plan needs one coherent path, e.g. construct the driver with adapters built from the requested `items_dir` and dispatch `TaskContractInput::Check`, or remove `items_dir` from the driver input and keep rendering outside it.

The spec and implementation plan disagree on the workflow command being gated: IN-05/AC-03/AC-06 require the gate before/blocking `sotp review local`, while T008 wires only `bin/sotp review fix-local` via `track-local-review-fix-codex`. Because T008 is marked as satisfying those criteria, the artifacts can claim AC-06 coverage while implementing a different review invocation path. Align the spec criteria with `review fix-local` or change T008 to gate the command named by the spec.

The dependency narrative lets T005 run before the composition root it calls exists. T005 explicitly constructs `TaskContractCompositionRoot` and calls `task_contract_check`, but the section says T005 depends only on T004 while T006 is the task that adds `TaskContractCompositionRoot`. A task runner following this plan can schedule/assign T005 before T006 and produce an unbuildable CLI task; add a T005 dependency on T006 or reorder the composition task before the CLI execute task.

`ImplCatalogSignalReaderPort` is traced to `spec.json#IN-02`, but IN-02 is the impl-planner responsibility to author `task-contract.json`. This port reads existing per-layer impl_catalog signal documents, so the traceability should point to the gate/signal-join requirement such as IN-04/CN-05. The same mismatch appears on `FsImplCatalogSignalReader`, making the catalogue claim the signal-reader work satisfies the wrong spec element.

OS-01 says the gate input is rustdoc JSON, but IN-04/CN-05 and the Phase 2/3 artifacts define the gate as reading existing impl_catalog/type-signals documents and explicitly not changing SignalEvaluatorV2. That gives implementers two incompatible input contracts; following OS-01 would push the design toward re-reading rustdoc instead of using the signal table, violating the intended signal-reuse boundary.

The rendered type views are inconsistent with the plan's completed state: `impl-plan.json` and `plan.md` mark T001/T003/T005 as done, but the matching artefacts still leave some newly added surfaces non-blue (`domain-types.md:14` keeps `ContractedEntryRef` đźźˇ; `cli-types.md:7-13` and `infrastructure-types.md:7-20` keep the new command/DTO/adapter entries đź”´). If those batches are meant to be finished, these views are stale and contradict the 'done' claim.

`commit_hash` was removed from the task records instead of being kept as `null` for the completed Batch A tasks. The briefing explicitly defines the lifecycle state as T001-T007 `done` with `commit_hash: null` until the D4 backfill records the batch commit; omitting the field means the plan artifact no longer represents that pending-backfill state and may leave the backfill step without a per-task slot to update.

T001 is marked `done` in `impl-plan.json`, and that task explicitly includes `PreReviewGateOutcome`, but the scoped type view still shows `PreReviewGateOutcome` with a Yellow implementation signal. That contradicts the plan's completed-state claim for T001 and leaves the plan asserting the batch is done while one of its contracted domain entries is non-Blue.

The lifecycle-tail briefing says this batch should be limited to T001-T007 status transitions plus the rendered plan, with type/SoT artifacts unchanged, but this scoped diff adds a new domain catalogue type (`PreReviewGateViolations`) and corresponding contract-map/docs updates. That silently changes the domain contract while the plan marks T001 done with no commit hash, so the artifact set no longer represents a pure Batch A status transition. Revert/split the type-catalogue changes or run them through the appropriate design/plan review path instead of bundling them with the lifecycle update.

T001's task text narrows the required domain inherent impls to TaskContractDocument accessors plus ContractedEntryRef::new, but domain-types.json also defines ContractedEntryRef::layer, ContractedEntryRef::entry_key, and PreReviewGateOutcome::blocked. Because ContractedEntryRef's fields are documented as stripped/private and Blocked construction is documented to go through blocked(), the plan artifact is internally inconsistent about the public API T001 must deliver.

T008 is marked `done`, but its `commit_hash` field is missing instead of being present as `null`. The plan's lifecycle contract for this batch is that T008 remains hashless until the later Batch B backfill, so the artifact should preserve `commit_hash: null` rather than dropping the field.

The generated plan still describes the task-contract API as `group`/`--group` in several places, but the updated catalogues now model it as optional `layer`/`--layer` (`usecase-types.json`, `cli_driver-types.json`, `cli_types.json`, `cli_composition-types.json`). In this file, T002/T004/T005/T006/T008 all keep the old `group` shape, so the plan is internally contradictory and the public CLI/service contract is ambiguous.

The new `read_optional_signals` contract says a missing `<layer>-type-signals.json` can be classified as `Ok(None)` and then â€śskip layers without a signal document silentlyâ€ť (`usecase-types.json`), but the surrounding plan artifacts still describe the gate as checking all 6 canonical layers in sequence and `FsImplCatalogSignalReader` as mapping read failures to `SignalReadFailed`. As written, a canonical layer with an absent signal file can be ignored instead of blocking the gate, so the all-layers mode is no longer actually fail-closed.

The ownership paragraph is stale: it still says this capability owns only `impl-plan.json` and `task-coverage.json`, but the same file now declares `task-contract.json` as a third Phase 3 artifact and allows writing it. That leaves the harness contract internally inconsistent about what `impl-planner` owns.

The new gate parses `--group`, but the actual review-fix wrapper contract still uses `--scope` on the caller side and `bin/sotp review fix-local` itself expects `--scope`. That means the pre-review gate is unreachable for the documented invocation path, and any invocation that does pass `--group` will be forwarded unchanged to `review fix-local` and fail there. The task-contract check/briefing append therefore does not wire into the real fix workflow.

Step 3 now makes surfaced PR-review comments non-terminal and requires fixing, committing, and re-running until an explicit zero-findings signal or user-approved Accepted Deviations. However, the stale Behavior summary still describes comments-surfaced as a reportable end state with only an actionability assessment and a next command. Agents following this summary can stop after the first non-pass round, which breaks the new MUST-continue loop contract. Update the Behavior section to report only terminal states and include the per-round trace/fix commits described above.

Step 3 still tells the operator to proceed to `/track:commit` after a zero-findings PR review, while the same command's terminal-state report now points to `/track:merge` once đź‘Ť is reached. That leaves two conflicting next-command wirings for the same terminal state; following the older `/track:commit` path after a clean PR review can send the workflow back to the guarded local commit command instead of the CI-gated PR merge flow described by this command and `/track:merge`. Update this terminal-state branch to match the new merge/report behavior.
